text: Baggage Policy This baggage policy explains the standard rules that apply to carry-on baggage, checked baggage, excess baggage, and special items carried on flights operated by the airline. Baggage allowance may vary by fare type, route, cabin purchased, frequent flyer tier, and any local legal requirements that apply to the journey. The allowance shown on the passenger's booking confirmation or electronic ticket is the controlling allowance for travel. Passengers are responsible for reviewing the allowance for each flight in their itinerary before arriving at the airport, especially when the itinerary includes separate tickets, codeshare flights, or travel on partner airlines. Carry-on baggage: Most passengers may bring one standard carry-on bag and one small personal item into the cabin. A personal item should fit under the seat in front of the passenger and may include a handbag, laptop bag, briefcase, or small backpack. The maximum size for standard carry-on baggage is 56 cm x 36 cm x 23 cm, including wheels, side pockets, and handles. Carry-on baggage must be light enough for the passenger to lift and store without assistance unless assistance is required because of a disability. If overhead bin space becomes limited, the airline may require some carry-on baggage to be checked at the gate without prior notice. Cabin baggage that exceeds the applicable size or weight limit may be refused at the boarding gate and checked subject to any applicable fee. Checked baggage: On many economy fares, one checked bag up to 23 kg may be included, while premium cabins or higher-status passengers may receive a larger allowance. Some basic or promotional fares do not include checked baggage and require a separate purchase. Each checked bag must also comply with the airline's maximum linear dimension limit, usually measured by adding length, width, and height. Bags that exceed size

premium cabins or higher-status passengers may receive a larger allowance. Some basic or promotional fares do not include checked baggage and require a separate purchase. Each checked bag must also comply with the airline's maximum linear dimension limit, usually measured by adding length, width, and height. Bags that exceed size or weight limits may incur oversize or overweight fees in addition to any charge for extra bags. For safety reasons, the airline will not accept a single checked item weighing more than 32 kg. Passengers with heavier items must repack the contents into separate bags before check-in. Additional, overweight, and oversize baggage: Fees apply when a passenger checks more bags than the allowance included in the ticket, or when a checked bag exceeds the standard size or weight limit. Additional bag charges, overweight charges, and oversize charges are cumulative unless local law requires otherwise. Prepaid baggage rates purchased online before airport check-in are usually lower than airport rates. Published excess baggage charges may vary by route, currency, and season, and are subject to change until ticketing. Excess baggage is accepted only when space and weight limits on the flight permit. During peak travel periods, weather disruptions, or aircraft changes, the airline may limit the number of extra bags accepted on a flight. Special items: Sports equipment, musical instruments, medical equipment, strollers, child restraint systems, and certain mobility aids may be accepted under separate conditions. Some special items count toward the standard checked baggage allowance, while others are carried free of charge when required for accessibility or infant travel. Large sporting items, such as surfboards, bicycles, skis, or golf equipment, may require advance notice and may be subject to dedicated handling fees. Musical instruments that fit safely in the cabin may be carried as carry-on baggage if they meet cabin rules.

charge when required for accessibility or infant travel. Large sporting items, such as surfboards, bicycles, skis, or golf equipment, may require advance notice and may be subject to dedicated handling fees. Musical instruments that fit safely in the cabin may be carried as carry-on baggage if they meet cabin rules. Larger instruments may require the purchase of an additional seat or transport as checked baggage in an approved protective case. Restricted and prohibited items: Passengers must not place dangerous goods, prohibited articles, or items restricted by aviation security rules in checked or carry-on baggage except as specifically permitted. Spare lithium batteries, power banks, electronic cigarettes, and certain medical devices are subject to special carriage restrictions. Fragile, perishable, or high-value items such as jewelry, cash, negotiable documents, and important medication should be carried in the cabin and remain the passenger's responsibility. The airline may refuse baggage that is improperly packed, leaks, emits odor, appears unsafe to handle, or could damage other baggage or the aircraft. Passenger responsibilities: Passengers must ensure that all baggage is packed securely, clearly labeled with current contact information, and presented for check-in before the baggage acceptance deadline. Any bag accepted without a lock remains subject to security inspection by airport or government authorities. Where a lock is used, passengers should use an approved lock type where required and understand that security authorities may open the bag for inspection. Baggage checked on one flight may not automatically be through-checked to a final destination when travel includes separate tickets, overnight stops, customs re-clearance, or partner carriers with different rules. Passengers are responsible for collecting and rechecking baggage whenever instructed to do so. Infants, children, and special assistance: Passengers traveling with an infant may usually check a stroller or collapsible pushchair and, on eligible fares, one additional infant item such

overnight stops, customs re-clearance, or partner carriers with different rules. Passengers are responsible for collecting and rechecking baggage whenever instructed to do so. Infants, children, and special assistance: Passengers traveling with an infant may usually check a stroller or collapsible pushchair and, on eligible fares, one additional infant item such as a car seat. Mobility aids used by passengers with disabilities are carried according to applicable accessibility obligations and safety requirements. The airline may ask for battery information, dimensions, or handling instructions in advance for electric wheelchairs or similar devices. Passengers who require special baggage handling or extra time at the airport should notify the airline as early as possible before departure. Delayed, damaged, or missing baggage: If checked baggage is delayed, damaged, or missing on arrival, the passenger should report the issue to the airline's baggage service desk before leaving the airport whenever possible. A written report may be required within the time limit specified by applicable conventions or local law. Passengers should retain baggage tags, boarding passes, receipts, and photographs of any damage. Reimbursement for essential purchases during a baggage delay may be limited to reasonable and documented expenses. The airline's liability for lost, delayed, or damaged baggage is limited by applicable law and international conventions unless a higher declared value has been accepted where available. Important note: Baggage rules can differ by route, fare family, and operating carrier. Passengers should review the baggage allowance on their booking confirmation before travel and allow extra time for payment, inspection, or repacking if traveling with additional or special baggage.

Cancellation Policy This cancellation policy explains how passengers may cancel a booking before travel and what consequences may apply after cancellation. Cancellation rules depend on the fare purchased, the route, the time remaining before departure, and whether the cancellation is voluntary or caused by a disruption outside the passenger's control. The specific fare conditions attached to the booking govern the outcome of a cancellation request. Passengers should review those conditions at the time of booking because low promotional fares often carry stricter cancellation penalties than flexible fares. How to cancel: Passengers may cancel an eligible booking through the airline website, mobile app, customer support center, airport ticket desk, or the original travel agency that issued the ticket. The cancellation must be completed before the published departure time of the first unused flight on the itinerary unless the fare rules specifically permit a later request. A request started but not fully confirmed before departure may not prevent the booking from being treated as a no-show. Where a reservation includes multiple passengers, the person making the cancellation should ensure the request clearly identifies whether all travelers or only selected travelers are being cancelled. Cancellation before departure: If cancellation is made before departure, the ticket may be cancelled with no penalty, with a published cancellation fee, or without any residual value, depending on the fare family. Flexible and fully refundable fares generally allow cancellation with little or no penalty, while basic and discounted fares often carry a fixed fee or complete forfeiture of the base fare. In many cases, taxes or government charges for unused travel may still be refundable even when the fare itself is not. Optional purchases such as paid seats, priority boarding, lounge access, prepaid baggage, and onboard meal selections are usually non-refundable unless the airline is unable to provide

the base fare. In many cases, taxes or government charges for unused travel may still be refundable even when the fare itself is not. Optional purchases such as paid seats, priority boarding, lounge access, prepaid baggage, and onboard meal selections are usually non-refundable unless the airline is unable to provide the service purchased. Risk of no-show: If a passenger does not cancel before the flight departs and also does not board, the booking may be classified as a no-show. A no-show may result in loss of the entire ticket value, automatic cancellation of all remaining flights in the same itinerary, and forfeiture of any onward or return segments. Some fares also impose a separate no-show penalty in addition to standard cancellation fees. Passengers who know they will miss their flight should cancel as soon as possible to preserve any remaining value allowed under the fare rules. Travel credits after cancellation: When a cancelled fare is not eligible for cash refund but retains residual value, the airline may issue a travel credit. Travel credits are usually valid for a limited period measured from the original ticket issue date or the original departure date, depending on fare rules. Credits are commonly non-transferable and must be used by the same passenger named on the original ticket. Any future booking made with a credit may still require payment of a fare difference, taxes, or service fee. If the new itinerary is cheaper than the credit value, some fare types preserve the remaining balance while others forfeit the unused amount. Cooling-off and legally required cancellation rights: Certain markets may require a short period after booking during which a passenger may cancel without penalty, provided the booking was made sufficiently far in advance of departure. Where such legal rights apply, the airline will follow the applicable

others forfeit the unused amount. Cooling-off and legally required cancellation rights: Certain markets may require a short period after booking during which a passenger may cancel without penalty, provided the booking was made sufficiently far in advance of departure. Where such legal rights apply, the airline will follow the applicable law for that market. Outside those legally required circumstances, the airline is not obliged to provide a free cancellation period unless the fare conditions specifically include one. Passengers should not assume that a booking can always be cancelled without penalty simply because the request is made shortly after purchase. Special circumstances: Requests to cancel due to serious illness, death of an immediate family member, military orders, jury service, visa refusal, or other exceptional events may be reviewed on a case-by-case basis if supporting documents are provided. Approval of an exception is not automatic and may be limited to a waiver of the cancellation fee, issuance of a travel credit, or refund of only the unused taxes and charges. The airline may require original or certified documents and may refuse documents submitted after an unreasonable delay. Group bookings, charter fares, and tickets issued under corporate or tour contracts may follow separate cancellation terms. Bookings with partner airlines: If an itinerary includes flights operated by another airline, cancellation of the overall booking may be restricted by the most restrictive fare rule in the itinerary. Some partner segments cannot be partially cancelled online and may require assistance from customer support or the original issuing agent. Cancelling one sector of a multi-city or return itinerary may cause the remaining fare to be recalculated, which can reduce or eliminate any residual value. Passenger responsibilities: Passengers are responsible for verifying that the cancellation confirmation has been issued and for keeping a copy of the confirmation email,

issuing agent. Cancelling one sector of a multi-city or return itinerary may cause the remaining fare to be recalculated, which can reduce or eliminate any residual value. Passenger responsibilities: Passengers are responsible for verifying that the cancellation confirmation has been issued and for keeping a copy of the confirmation email, reference number, and any travel credit details. If payment was made through a third-party agency, the airline may not be able to confirm how long that agency takes to process the cancellation. Passengers must also ensure they comply with any deadline for cancelling ancillary services, airport hotel packages, or separately booked ground transport. Important note: Cancellation and refund are related but not identical. A ticket may be successfully cancelled while still producing only a partial refund, a travel credit, or no remaining value at all. Passengers should review fare rules carefully before cancelling.

Check-In Policy This check-in policy explains the deadlines and passenger responsibilities that apply before boarding. Check-in is the process by which the airline verifies the passenger's reservation, travel documents, baggage, and readiness to travel. Completing check-in on time is essential. A confirmed reservation does not guarantee travel if the passenger fails to meet check-in, baggage drop, document verification, security, or boarding deadlines. Different airports and routes may have different cutoff times, and the times shown in the booking confirmation or on the airline website are the deadlines that apply to the specific flight. Online and mobile check-in: Online and mobile check-in typically open 24 hours before scheduled departure, although some routes may open earlier or later due to airport, government, or document requirements. On eligible itineraries, passengers may select seats, confirm contact details, and receive a mobile or printable boarding pass during this period. Online check-in may not be available if document checks are required at the airport, if the booking involves an unaccompanied minor, pet travel, extra seat purchase, special medical clearance, or certain international destinations with visa verification requirements. A passenger who checks in online must still comply with baggage drop and boarding deadlines. Checking in online does not remove the need to present required documents on request. Airport check-in and baggage drop: Passengers who need to check baggage, complete document verification, or receive special assistance should arrive at the airport with enough time to complete all formalities. Airport check-in counters and bag drop facilities may open and close at fixed times based on local operating conditions. In general, domestic services may require check-in and bag drop to be completed at least 45 to 60 minutes before departure, while international services may require completion 60 to 90 minutes before departure or earlier on certain routes. Some airports operate

close at fixed times based on local operating conditions. In general, domestic services may require check-in and bag drop to be completed at least 45 to 60 minutes before departure, while international services may require completion 60 to 90 minutes before departure or earlier on certain routes. Some airports operate self-service kiosks or dedicated bag drop counters, but availability of these facilities does not extend the published deadline. Baggage presented after the cutoff time may be refused even if the passenger has already checked in online. Document requirements: Passengers are solely responsible for carrying valid passports, visas, residence permits, health certificates, and any other documents required by the departure, transit, and destination authorities. The airline may refuse check-in or boarding if documents are missing, inconsistent with the booking, damaged, or invalid for travel. The airline may also request proof of onward travel, local contact details, or additional entry documentation where required by government authorities. A boarding pass issued online or at a kiosk remains subject to cancellation if document inspection later shows that the passenger does not meet travel requirements. Seat assignment and standby: Seat assignments issued during check-in remain subject to operational change, aircraft substitution, safety requirements, and rebalancing of the aircraft cabin. Passengers who fail to present themselves at the boarding gate by the required time may lose their reserved seat even if they completed check-in on time. In overbooking or disruption situations, the airline may manage standby and seat allocation according to operational needs, status benefits, fare rules, and applicable denied boarding procedures. Completing check-in early does not guarantee protection from all seat changes, but late check-in increases the risk that preferred seating or even travel on the booked flight may no longer be available. Late arrival and refusal of travel: If a passenger arrives after the

rules, and applicable denied boarding procedures. Completing check-in early does not guarantee protection from all seat changes, but late check-in increases the risk that preferred seating or even travel on the booked flight may no longer be available. Late arrival and refusal of travel: If a passenger arrives after the published check-in deadline, the airline may refuse travel on that flight and treat the booking as a no-show according to the fare rules. The same outcome may apply when the passenger arrives at security or the boarding gate too late, even if check-in was completed earlier. The airline is not responsible for delays caused by road traffic, parking shortages, long airport queues, or late arrival at the wrong terminal. Passengers should allow additional time during school holidays, severe weather, airport construction, or heightened security conditions. Special cases: Passengers requiring wheelchair assistance, carriage of mobility aids, pet travel, unaccompanied minor handling, infant travel documents, carriage of firearms where permitted, or transport of oversized or special baggage should contact the airline in advance and arrive earlier than standard recommendation. Some requests must be approved before airport check-in can be completed. Passengers traveling on separate tickets are responsible for confirming whether baggage can be through-checked and whether additional document or immigration clearance will be required between flights. Passenger responsibilities: Passengers must verify their flight time before leaving for the airport because schedule changes, terminal changes, and gate changes can occur. They should ensure that names on the booking match travel documents, that baggage complies with published rules, and that contact details remain current. After check-in, passengers must proceed through security and be present at the gate no later than the boarding time shown on the boarding pass or announced by staff. Failure to comply with staff instructions, security screening requirements, or boarding deadlines

complies with published rules, and that contact details remain current. After check-in, passengers must proceed through security and be present at the gate no later than the boarding time shown on the boarding pass or announced by staff. Failure to comply with staff instructions, security screening requirements, or boarding deadlines may result in denied travel without refund beyond what is allowed in the fare conditions. Important note: Published check-in deadlines are strict operational deadlines, not recommended arrival times. Passengers should arrive well before the cutoff and allow enough time for queues, document checks, baggage processing, security screening, and walking time to the gate.

Delay Compensation Policy This policy describes the assistance, reimbursement, and compensation options that may apply when a flight is delayed. The support available to passengers depends on the length of the delay, the route involved, the reason for the disruption, the applicable law, and whether the flight is operated by the airline or by a partner carrier. Not every delay qualifies for compensation. In many cases, the airline's obligation is limited to providing updates, reasonable care during the disruption, and rebooking assistance rather than a cash payment. Flight status updates: When a delay is identified, the airline will generally communicate revised departure information by airport display screens, boarding announcements, email, SMS, or the mobile app using the contact details attached to the booking. Passengers are responsible for ensuring their contact information is accurate and for monitoring airport announcements during travel. Published departure times may continue to change as operational conditions evolve, and an estimated time of departure should not be interpreted as a guarantee. Even when notifications are sent, passengers should remain at the gate area or follow airport instructions because boarding can begin on short notice once the aircraft is ready. Minor delays: For shorter delays, the airline will typically provide operational updates and, where possible, revised boarding times. Minor delays do not automatically trigger compensation, reimbursement, or access to meals, hotel accommodation, or ground transport. Passengers should avoid purchasing replacement tickets or additional travel services unless instructed to do so by the airline, because voluntary purchases made without authorization may not be reimbursed. Extended delays and passenger care: If a delay becomes significant, the airline may provide reasonable care based on the circumstances, local law, and availability at the airport. Reasonable care may include meal vouchers, refreshments, communication assistance, hotel accommodation for an overnight delay, or transport between

authorization may not be reimbursed. Extended delays and passenger care: If a delay becomes significant, the airline may provide reasonable care based on the circumstances, local law, and availability at the airport. Reasonable care may include meal vouchers, refreshments, communication assistance, hotel accommodation for an overnight delay, or transport between the airport and an approved hotel. Such support is generally provided only when the delay is within the airline's control or when required by law. Hotel rooms are subject to availability and may be limited near the airport during severe weather or broad operational disruptions. Passengers who choose to arrange their own hotel or meals without prior approval may be reimbursed only for reasonable documented expenses and only when the policy or law allows it. Rebooking and rerouting: When a delay causes a misconnection or materially disrupts the passenger's itinerary, the airline may rebook the passenger on the next available flight operated by the airline, or in some cases on a partner airline or an alternative routing. If the passenger accepts an alternative flight, cash compensation rights may be reduced or removed depending on the applicable law and the delay eventually experienced at the final destination. If no acceptable alternative is available, eligible passengers may request a refund of the unused portion of the ticket under the refund policy. Rebooking options may be limited by seat availability, immigration restrictions, airport curfews, or airport security requirements. Compensation eligibility: Cash or fixed compensation is not automatically available for every delay. Eligibility generally depends on whether the disruption was caused by factors within the airline's control, the length of the final arrival delay, and the passenger's compliance with check-in and boarding requirements. Delays caused by severe weather, air traffic control restrictions, airport security incidents, political instability, labor action outside the airline's control, public

depends on whether the disruption was caused by factors within the airline's control, the length of the final arrival delay, and the passenger's compliance with check-in and boarding requirements. Delays caused by severe weather, air traffic control restrictions, airport security incidents, political instability, labor action outside the airline's control, public health measures, or safety-related events may not qualify for monetary compensation even when the delay is lengthy. Delays caused by operational issues within the airline's control may qualify for compensation only on routes or in markets where a law, regulation, or published customer commitment provides such payment. Claim requirements: Passengers seeking reimbursement or compensation should retain boarding passes, booking confirmations, baggage receipts, and itemized receipts for any approved out-of-pocket expenses. Claims may need to be submitted within a specified time after travel, and incomplete claims may be delayed or rejected. The airline may request additional information to verify the cause of the disruption, the amount claimed, proof of travel, and proof that the expense was necessary and reasonable. Expenses relating to alcohol, luxury accommodation, upgraded transportation, or speculative onward losses are usually not reimbursable unless required by law. Limitations and exclusions: The airline is not responsible for indirect or consequential losses arising from a delayed flight except where applicable law provides otherwise. This includes missed business opportunities, lost wages, missed events, separate ticket failures, or expenses caused by the passenger's independent arrangements. If the passenger chooses not to travel after a delay but declines the rebooking options or assistance offered, compensation and reimbursement may be limited. Any compensation already paid under one regime may be deducted from amounts owed under another regime to prevent duplicate recovery. Important note: Passengers should present themselves for check-in and boarding on time even when a delay has been announced, because eligibility for support and

compensation and reimbursement may be limited. Any compensation already paid under one regime may be deducted from amounts owed under another regime to prevent duplicate recovery. Important note: Passengers should present themselves for check-in and boarding on time even when a delay has been announced, because eligibility for support and compensation may be lost if the passenger fails to comply with operational instructions. Keeping travel documents and receipts will help the airline review any later claim.

Flight Change Policy This flight change policy explains when a passenger may change the date, time, or routing of an existing booking. Flight changes are subject to fare conditions, seat availability in the applicable booking class, operational limits, and payment of any difference between the original fare and the new fare selected. Some tickets permit changes with low or no service fee, while others do not permit voluntary changes at all. The fare rules attached to the ticket remain the controlling terms for any requested change. Eligibility to change a booking: Passengers may request a change before the scheduled departure time of the first unused flight coupon unless a different deadline is stated in the fare rules. Requests made after departure are generally not permitted except on fares specifically marked as changeable after departure. A change request is valid only when the passenger has received confirmation and any required additional payment has been collected. Starting a change request online without completing payment or confirmation does not preserve the original fare or prevent the ticket from becoming a no-show if the original flight departs. Advance flight changes: For changes made before the day of departure, the passenger may need to pay both a change fee and any fare difference between the original ticket and the newly selected itinerary. If the new flight is cheaper, the treatment of the remaining value depends on the fare rules. Some fares preserve a residual credit, while others do not refund any difference. Changes may also require the ticket to be reissued in the same cabin or fare family, and promotional inventory may not be available on the new date. If the new itinerary involves different airports, a longer route, or a different carrier, additional taxes, airport charges, or partner airline fees may apply. Same-day changes:

require the ticket to be reissued in the same cabin or fare family, and promotional inventory may not be available on the new date. If the new itinerary involves different airports, a longer route, or a different carrier, additional taxes, airport charges, or partner airline fees may apply. Same-day changes: Passengers traveling on eligible fares may request a same-day confirmed change to an earlier or later flight on the same calendar day as the original departure. Same-day changes are usually limited to the same origin and destination, the same passenger, and the same operating carrier. A published same-day change fee may apply unless waived by status benefits or premium fare conditions. Some passengers may instead be offered same-day standby, which does not guarantee a seat and is processed according to airport and operational procedures. Same-day changes are subject to seat availability and may be restricted for international sectors, flights involving security screening deadlines, or itineraries that include checked baggage already accepted on the original flight. Restricted fares and exceptions: Basic economy, promotional sale fares, group fares, charter tickets, and some tickets issued by travel agents may not permit voluntary changes. In those cases, the passenger may need to cancel the booking under the applicable cancellation rules and purchase a new ticket if permitted. Certain compassionate or disruption-related exceptions may be offered when a change is requested because of medical emergency, bereavement, or official travel restrictions, but documentation may be required and approval is not automatic. The airline may also refuse a change if the passenger has not met payment conditions, ticket validity rules, or visa and entry requirements for the new itinerary. Changes to connecting itineraries: When a booking contains multiple sectors on the same ticket, changing one segment may require the entire itinerary to be repriced. This can affect

a change if the passenger has not met payment conditions, ticket validity rules, or visa and entry requirements for the new itinerary. Changes to connecting itineraries: When a booking contains multiple sectors on the same ticket, changing one segment may require the entire itinerary to be repriced. This can affect both the fare owed and the baggage allowance attached to the ticket. If a passenger asks to remove a connecting sector or stop over in a city not originally permitted by the fare, the new itinerary may be priced as a completely separate journey. Changes involving partner-operated or codeshare flights may require manual review and can take longer to confirm. Some partner fares do not allow partial changes once travel has begun. Ancillary services and seat assignments: Additional services purchased for the original flight, including checked baggage, seat selection, lounge access, onboard Wi-Fi, or special meals, may not automatically transfer to the new booking. Where possible, the airline will transfer the service or provide comparable accommodation, but some services may need to be reselected or repurchased. If the passenger fails to request a transfer before departure, unused optional services may become non-refundable according to their own terms. Passengers needing wheelchair assistance, unaccompanied minor service, pet travel, or medical clearance should contact the airline promptly after making a change so that the new flight can be reviewed for suitability. Passenger responsibilities: Passengers are responsible for checking that the revised itinerary, fare collection, seat assignment, and baggage allowance are correct after a change is processed. Change confirmations should be reviewed immediately, especially when the new itinerary includes overnight connections, airport changes within the same city, or separate tickets. The passenger remains responsible for meeting all check-in deadlines for the new flight and for ensuring that passports, visas, transit permissions, and health documents

change is processed. Change confirmations should be reviewed immediately, especially when the new itinerary includes overnight connections, airport changes within the same city, or separate tickets. The passenger remains responsible for meeting all check-in deadlines for the new flight and for ensuring that passports, visas, transit permissions, and health documents remain valid for the revised travel dates. Important note: All voluntary flight changes must be finalized before the relevant deadline in the fare rules. A request is not complete until the airline has confirmed the new itinerary and collected any applicable fee or fare difference.

Refund Policy This refund policy explains when a passenger may receive a refund for an unused or partially used ticket and how refunds are processed. Refund eligibility depends on the fare rules attached to the ticket, the reason the refund is requested, the route traveled, the method of payment, and any local laws that provide additional consumer rights. The ticket conditions displayed at the time of booking remain the primary source for determining whether a refund is permitted. Passengers should review those fare conditions carefully before purchasing, because some fares are intentionally sold at a lower price in exchange for strict refund limits. Voluntary refunds: If a passenger chooses not to travel and cancels a booking before the scheduled departure of the first unused flight coupon, a refund may be available if the fare purchased is refundable. Refundable fares may be refunded to the original form of payment after deduction of any applicable cancellation fee, service charge, used portion of the itinerary, or non-refundable taxes and surcharges where permitted. Some fares allow a full refund only when cancellation is completed a specified number of hours or days before departure. If the passenger cancels after that deadline, a reduced refund or a travel credit may apply instead. Voluntary refund requests must be made by the passenger named on the ticket or by the person who paid for the booking if the airline requires payment verification. Non-refundable tickets: Most basic, promotional, or deeply discounted fares are non-refundable. When a non-refundable ticket is cancelled before departure, the ticket value may be retained as a travel credit if the fare rules specifically allow credit issuance. Any such credit is usually non-transferable, must be used within the validity period stated on the booking, and may only be applied toward future travel issued in the same

cancelled before departure, the ticket value may be retained as a travel credit if the fare rules specifically allow credit issuance. Any such credit is usually non-transferable, must be used within the validity period stated on the booking, and may only be applied toward future travel issued in the same currency or country of sale. Service fees, seat selection charges, baggage fees, carbon offset contributions, and other optional purchases are generally non-refundable unless required by law or unless the related service could not be provided by the airline. Involuntary refunds: An involuntary refund may be offered when the airline cancels a flight, fails to operate a flight reasonably according to schedule, causes a significant schedule change, or is unable to provide the class of service purchased and the passenger does not accept an offered alternative. In those circumstances, the passenger may usually request a refund for the unused ticket portion. If travel has already begun and the disruption affects the remaining itinerary, the passenger may also request a refund of the unused balance and, where applicable, the difference between the fare paid and the transportation actually provided. Eligibility may depend on whether the passenger accepted rebooking, completed part of the journey voluntarily, or used the ticket after being informed of the schedule change. Partially used tickets: When only part of an itinerary has been used, the refund amount is not automatically equal to the unused sector price shown in the booking path. The airline may recalculate the value of the transportation already used based on the fare conditions that applied to the flown sectors and deduct that amount from the total ticket price. If the remaining balance after recalculation is zero, no refund is due. Certain taxes, fees, or government charges associated with unused sectors may still be refundable even

transportation already used based on the fare conditions that applied to the flown sectors and deduct that amount from the total ticket price. If the remaining balance after recalculation is zero, no refund is due. Certain taxes, fees, or government charges associated with unused sectors may still be refundable even where the base fare is not. Refunds of unused airport or passenger service charges may require a separate request. Refund request process: Tickets purchased directly from the airline should normally be refunded through the airline's website, call center, ticket office, or customer support channels. Tickets purchased through a travel agency, online travel platform, or other third party usually must be refunded by that original issuing agent unless the airline advises otherwise. The passenger may be asked to provide booking details, contact information, banking confirmation, proof of identity, and documents supporting the reason for the request. Refund claims based on illness, bereavement, duplicate ticketing, or visa refusal may require official evidence and will be reviewed under the relevant fare rules or compassionate policy, if available. Refund timing: Approved refunds are typically processed within 7 to 14 business days for credit card purchases and may take longer for cash, bank transfer, voucher, or third-party payment methods. Processing times can also increase during major disruptions or periods of unusually high claim volume. The airline's processing time refers only to the time required to approve and release the refund. Posting times for the passenger's bank, card issuer, or payment provider are outside the airline's control. If a refund is issued in a currency different from the original transaction currency, exchange rate differences and bank charges may reduce the amount received. Non-refundable amounts and exceptions: Some taxes, supplier fees, insurance premiums, booking fees, and optional services remain non-refundable even on refundable tickets. A ticket used

control. If a refund is issued in a currency different from the original transaction currency, exchange rate differences and bank charges may reduce the amount received. Non-refundable amounts and exceptions: Some taxes, supplier fees, insurance premiums, booking fees, and optional services remain non-refundable even on refundable tickets. A ticket used in violation of fare rules, obtained fraudulently, charged back without basis, or altered after issuance may be denied refund in full or in part. No-show passengers who fail to cancel before departure may lose the right to any refund or credit unless the fare rules state otherwise. When a passenger is denied boarding or prevented from traveling because required travel documents were missing, invalid, or expired, the refund will be handled according to the underlying fare rules and may be limited. Important note: Submitting a refund request does not guarantee approval. Passengers should cancel any booking they do not intend to use before departure, keep copies of their confirmation and receipts, and allow sufficient time for review where documents are required.
